projects.title: Unit 3 Project — Ratios in Action
projects.desc: Capstone project: apply ratio reasoning to a real-world challenge.

projects.title: Ratio Lab
projects.desc: Build and explore part-to-part and part-to-whole ratios hands-on.

projects.title: Ratio Concept Game (6.RP.1)
projects.desc: Practice writing and describing ratios that compare two quantities.

projects.title: Unit 3 Graphic Novel
projects.desc: Follow the story and see ratios used in context.

title: Understand Ratios
contentObjective: I can write and describe a ratio that compares two quantities.
languageObjective: I can explain a comparison using the words ratio, part-to-part, part-to-whole, and colon notation.

turnAndTalk.question: Chef Reyes's signature cookie recipe uses 3 cups of flour for every 2 cups of sugar. How would you describe how these two ingredient amounts compare?
turnAndTalk.kernel: The recipe uses 3 cups of flour for every 2 cups of sugar.

stems.en: For every ___ cups of flour there are ___ cups of sugar.
stems.es: Por cada ___ tazas de harina hay ___ tazas de azúcar.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: A strong answer names that flour and sugar are being compared and states the relationship as 3 to 2 (or 'for every 3 flour, 2 sugar'), not just that there is more flour.
turnAndTalk.extend: Would the ratio still describe the same recipe if you wrote it as sugar to flour instead of flour to sugar? Justify why the order changes the ratio but not the recipe.
extendStems: The order matters because ___, so flour to sugar (___) is different from sugar to flour (___).
Both describe the same recipe because ___.

turnAndTalk.question: At the Prep Station you sorted the chef's statements. What made something a ratio instead of NOT a ratio (like '12 cookies on the tray')?
turnAndTalk.kernel: A ratio compares two quantities, but '12 cookies' is only one amount.

stems.en: This is a ratio because it compares ___ to ___.
stems.es: Esto es una razón porque compara ___ con ___.

stems.en: This is NOT a ratio because it only ___.
stems.es: Esto NO es una razón porque solo ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Listen for the idea that a ratio must compare two quantities (using 'to', 'for every', 'out of', or a colon), while a single count or a description is not a comparison.
turnAndTalk.extend: Critique this claim: 'The oven is set to 350°F is a ratio because it has the word to in it.' Is the student right? Defend your answer.
extendStems: The student is wrong because the word 'to' alone does not ___; a ratio must ___.
350°F is just ___, not a comparison of ___ and ___.

turnAndTalk.question: In Menu Planning the cafeteria wants 3 main dishes for every 2 side dishes. How is this the same kind of thinking as Chef Reyes's flour-to-sugar ratio?
turnAndTalk.kernel: Both use a ratio to keep two amounts in the same comparison.

stems.en: This is like our ratio work because ___ and ___ are compared.
stems.es: Esto es como nuestro trabajo con razones porque se comparan ___ y ___.

stems.en: The ratio of main dishes to side dishes is ___.
stems.es: La razón de platos principales a guarniciones es ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: A strong answer identifies the 3:2 part-to-part ratio and explains that the cafeteria keeps that comparison the same even when amounts grow, just like the recipe.
turnAndTalk.extend: If the cafeteria has 10 side dishes, generalize how you could figure out the main dishes — and explain why simply 'adding 1 more main than side' would NOT work.
extendStems: I can scale the ratio 3:2 by ___, so 10 sides means ___ mains.
Adding 1 each time does not work because a ratio must ___, not ___.

turnAndTalk.question: During practice on Understand Ratios, what strategy did you use when a problem felt tricky?
turnAndTalk.kernel: A ratio compares two quantities, and the order you write them in matters.

stems.en: This is a ratio because it compares ___ to ___.
stems.es: Esto es una razón porque compara ___ con ___.

stems.en: I knew my answer was reasonable because ___.
stems.es: Supe que mi respuesta era razonable porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Listen for students naming a specific strategy tied to 6.RP.1 — not just "I multiplied." They should connect steps to the key idea.
turnAndTalk.extend: How would you help a classmate who made the common mistake from today's lesson? Use one vocabulary word.
extendStems: I would tell them to ___ first because ___.
The mistake happens when you ___ instead of ___.

mission.title: The Signature Recipe
mission.story: You just earned your apron at Chef Academy. Head Chef Reyes is trusting you with her signature cookie recipe — 3 cups of flour for every 2 cups of sugar. Before service begins, you must read every recipe like a pro by mastering ratios, or the whole kitchen falls behind.
mission.objective: Compare ingredient amounts using ratios so every batch comes out exactly right.

scenes.name: Recipe Card
scenes.text: Read Chef Reyes's recipe and notice how flour and sugar compare.

scenes.name: Kitchen Glossary
scenes.text: Learn the language of ratios: comparison, part-to-part, part-to-whole, and colon notation.

scenes.name: Prep Station
scenes.text: Sort the chef's statements — which are real ratios and which are not?

scenes.name: Cookie Line
scenes.text: Fill the cookie orders. The kitchen adapts the difficulty to keep you cooking.

scenes.name: Menu Planning
scenes.text: Use ratios to scale a real cafeteria menu of mains and sides.

scenes.name: Service Review
scenes.text: Plate your final dish and show Chef Reyes what you learned.

finale.title: Service Approved!
finale.text: Every batch came out balanced and the dining room is full. Chef Reyes just named you sous-chef of Chef Academy — all because you can read a recipe in ratios.

vocabulary.definition: A way to compare two amounts, like 3 to 2.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una manera de comparar dos cantidades, como 3 a 2.
vocabulary.cloze: A comparison of two quantities, like 3 to 5, is a ___.

examples.why: A single number does not compare two quantities.

vocabulary.definition: Looking at two or more amounts to see how they are related.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Mirar dos o más cantidades para ver cómo se relacionan.
vocabulary.visual: 5 eggs vs. 3 eggs — which recipe uses more?
vocabulary.cloze: Showing how two amounts relate to each other is a ___.

vocabulary.definition: A ratio comparing one part of a group to another part.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una razón que compara una parte de un grupo con otra parte.
vocabulary.visual: 3 cups flour : 2 cups sugar (ingredient to ingredient)
vocabulary.cloze: A ratio that compares one part of a group to another part is a ___ ratio.

examples.why: It compares one part to another part.
That compares a part to the whole.

vocabulary.definition: A ratio comparing one part to the whole group.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una razón que compara una parte con todo el grupo.
vocabulary.visual: 3 cups flour out of 5 cups total → 3:5
vocabulary.cloze: A ratio that compares one part of a group to the total is a ___ ratio.

examples.why: It compares a part to the whole group.
That compares a part to another part.

vocabulary.definition: Writing a ratio with two dots between the numbers, like 3:2.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Escribir una razón con dos puntos entre los números, como 3:2.
vocabulary.cloze: Writing a ratio with a colon, like 3:5, is using ___.

launch.narrative: Welcome to Chef Academy! Head Chef Reyes is teaching new students how to read recipes like a pro. Her signature cookie recipe calls for 3 cups of flour for every 2 cups of sugar. Before the students can start baking, they need to understand how these ingredient amounts compare to each other.
noticePrompts: What two ingredients are being compared in the recipe?
How could you describe the relationship between flour and sugar?
Is the amount of flour more than, less than, or equal to the amount of sugar?
wonderPrompts: What if we compared the sugar to the flour instead — would the ratio change?
Could we compare one ingredient to the total amount of both?
launch.contextImage: A kitchen counter with measuring cups showing 3 cups of flour and 2 cups of sugar beside a recipe card

conceptIntro.heading: What is a ratio?
conceptIntro.intro: A ratio compares two amounts. It tells how much of one thing there is for another thing.
conceptIntro.keyIdea: A ratio compares two quantities, and the order you write them in matters.

iDo.title: Watch me
lines: Chef Reyes uses 3 cups of flour for every 2 cups of sugar.
I am comparing two amounts: flour and sugar.
I write flour to sugar, in that order, as 3 to 2.
In colon notation I write it as 3:2.
So the ratio of flour to sugar is 3:2.

weDo.title: Let's try together
lines: A bowl has 4 apples and 1 orange. What two amounts are we comparing?
We write apples to oranges in order: 4 to 1.

youDo.title: Now it's your turn
lines: Next you will sort the chef's statements into ratios and not-ratios.
Remember: a real ratio compares two amounts.

explore.instructions: Chef Reyes wrote several comparisons on the board. Sort them into two groups: statements that ARE ratios and statements that are NOT ratios.

items.text: 4 eggs for every 1 stick of butter
5 red apples out of 8 total apples
There are 12 cookies on the tray

discourse.prompt: What makes something a ratio? How did you decide which statements to sort into each group?
discourse.sentenceFrame: A ratio compares ___ to ___ using words like ___ or symbols like ___.

approaching.stem: A recipe uses 4 cups of milk and 1 cup of cream. What is the ratio of milk to cream?
approaching.explanation: A ratio compares quantities in the order given. Milk to cream = 4 to 1, or 4:1.

approaching.stem: Chef Reyes uses 6 strawberries and 2 bananas in a smoothie. What is the part-to-whole ratio of bananas to total fruit?
approaching.explanation: Total fruit = 6 + 2 = 8. The part-to-whole ratio of bananas to total fruit is 2:8.

approaching.stem: In a class of 30 students, 18 are girls. What is the part-to-part ratio of boys to girls?
approaching.explanation: Boys = 30 − 18 = 12. Part-to-part ratio of boys to girls = 12:18. (Order matters!)

approaching.stem: A pet store has 5 dogs and 9 cats. What is the ratio of dogs to cats?
approaching.explanation: The ratio of dogs to cats compares dogs first, then cats: 5 to 9, or 5:9.

approaching.instructions: Sort each statement: does it describe a ratio or NOT describe a ratio?

items.text: There are 15 chairs in the room

approaching.title: approaching — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Understand Ratios.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: A ratio compares two quantities, and the order you write them in matters.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

approaching.correctWork: Follow the key idea: A ratio compares two quantities, and the order you write them in matters.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: A ratio compares two quantities, and the order you write them in matters.

onLevel.instructions: Sort each statement — is it a part-to-part ratio or a part-to-whole ratio?

items.text: 3 boys to 5 girls in cooking class
4 vegetarian dishes out of 10 total dishes
3 sweet items out of 10 total items

onLevel.stem: A fruit salad has 5 pieces of melon, 3 pieces of pineapple, and 2 grapes. What is the ratio of pineapple to melon?
onLevel.explanation: The ratio of pineapple to melon compares pineapple (3) to melon (5), so the ratio is 3:5.

onLevel.instructions: Complete the table by writing each ratio in all three forms.

onLevel.stem: A parking lot has 10 cars and 6 trucks. What is the part-to-whole ratio of trucks to total vehicles?
onLevel.explanation: Total vehicles = 10 + 6 = 16. The ratio of trucks to total is 6:16.

onLevel.title: onLevel — Spot the Common Mistake

workedExample.label: Read
workedExample.work: A student is working on Understand Ratios.

workedExample.label: Attempt
workedExample.work: They ignored the rule: A ratio compares two quantities, and the order you write them in matters.

workedExample.label: Check
workedExample.work: The answer does not match the math rule from class.

onLevel.correctWork: Follow the key idea: A ratio compares two quantities, and the order you write them in matters.
hints: Which step breaks the rule you learned today?
Remember: A ratio compares two quantities, and the order you write them in matters.

extending.title: Find Aliya's Mistake

workedExample.label: Read the problem
workedExample.work: A trail mix has 6 almonds, 4 raisins, and 2 cashews. Write the ratio of raisins to total pieces.

workedExample.label: Answer
workedExample.work: The ratio of raisins to total is 4:8.

extending.correctWork: The total is 12, not 8. The correct ratio is 4:12.
hints: Look at Step 3. Does 8 match the total Aliya found in Step 2?
The total is 12 pieces, so the part-to-whole ratio should use 12, not 8.

extending.stem: A bag of marbles has 8 red, 6 blue, and 4 green marbles. Write three different ratios using these quantities: one part-to-part ratio, one part-to-whole ratio, and explain the difference.
extending.sampleAnswer: Part-to-part: red to blue = 8:6. Part-to-whole: green to total = 4:18. The difference is that part-to-part compares two individual groups, while part-to-whole compares one group to the entire collection (8 + 6 + 4 = 18).

extending.instructions: A classroom has 12 boys and 8 girls. Sort each ratio statement as either CORRECT or INCORRECT.

optionalActivity.name: Culinary Ratio Challenge Room
optionalActivity.intro: Move through Chef Reyes's kitchen stations and prove you can read every recipe in ratios before service begins.

optional.stem: Station 1 — Sauce Bench: Chef Reyes mixes 3 cups of tomato sauce with 2 cups of cream. What is the ratio of tomato sauce to cream?
optional.explanation: A ratio compares the two amounts in the order named. Tomato sauce to cream = 3 to 2, written 3:2.

optional.stem: Station 2 — Fruit Tray: A tray holds 4 strawberries and 6 blueberries. What is the part-to-whole ratio of strawberries to total berries?
optional.explanation: Total berries = 4 + 6 = 10. The part-to-whole ratio of strawberries to total is 4:10.

optional.instructions: Station 3 — Recipe Board: Sort each kitchen comparison as a part-to-part ratio or a part-to-whole ratio.

items.text: 3 cups flour out of 5 cups total
4 sweet dishes out of 9 total dishes

optional.label: Station 4 — Plating Match: Match each kitchen picture or context to the ratio that describes it in colon form.

optional.stem: Station 5 — Soup Pot: A soup uses 8 cups of broth and 3 cups of noodles. What is the ratio of broth to noodles?
optional.explanation: Broth is named first, then noodles, so the ratio is 8 to 3, written 8:3.

optional.title: Level 2 Extension — Fix the Mislabeled Recipe Ratio

workedExample.label: Read the recipe
workedExample.work: Chef Reyes's salad uses 5 cups of lettuce and 3 cups of spinach. Write the part-to-part ratio of lettuce to spinach.

workedExample.label: Label the ratio
workedExample.work: This is a part-to-part ratio of lettuce to spinach.

optional.correctWork: Part-to-part compares lettuce to spinach, not lettuce to the total. The correct ratio is 5:3 (the 8 would only be used for a part-to-whole ratio like 5:8).
hints: Look at Step 3. A part-to-part ratio compares one part to another part — should the second number be the spinach amount or the total?
Lettuce to spinach uses 5 and 3, so the part-to-part ratio is 5:3. Use 8 only when comparing a part to the whole.
Now create your own recipe ratio: 'My recipe uses ___ cups of ___ to ___ cups of ___, so the part-to-part ratio is ___:___.'

practice.commonMistake: A common mistake in Understand Ratios is skipping the key idea: "A ratio compares two quantities, and the order you write them in matters." — always check your work against this rule before you submit.

connect.scenario: The school cafeteria is planning a new lunch menu. They want to offer 3 main dishes for every 2 side dishes. If they have 10 side dishes, the cafeteria manager needs to figure out how many main dishes to prepare.
connect.prompt: This is like our ratio work because ___ and ___ are related by ___.

exitTicket.stem: A bag of trail mix contains 8 peanuts and 5 raisins. What is the ratio of raisins to total pieces?
exitTicket.explanation: Total pieces = 8 + 5 = 13. The ratio of raisins to total is 5:13.

learningTonight.en: Compare ingredient amounts using ratios so every batch comes out exactly right.

conceptSteps.en: You just earned your apron at Chef Academy.
conceptSteps.es: Lean la historia de la misión juntos antes de empezar los ejercicios.

conceptSteps.en: A ratio compares two quantities, and the order you write them in matters.
conceptSteps.es: Verifiquen cada paso con la idea clave de la lección.

watchFor.en: Listen for "Ratio" — A way to compare two amounts, like 3 to 2.
watchFor.es: Escucha "Razón" — Una manera de comparar dos cantidades, como 3 a 2.

watchFor.en: Listen for "Comparison" — Looking at two or more amounts to see how they are related.
watchFor.es: Escucha "Comparación" — Mirar dos o más cantidades para ver cómo se relacionan.

watchFor.en: Listen for "Part-to-part" — A ratio comparing one part of a group to another part.
watchFor.es: Escucha "Parte a parte" — Una razón que compara una parte de un grupo con otra parte.

tryTogether.scenarioEn: You just earned your apron at Chef Academy. Head Chef Reyes is trusting you with her signature cookie recipe — 3 cups of flour for every 2 cups of sugar. Before service begins, you must read every recipe like a pro by mastering ratios, or the whole kitchen falls behind.
tryTogether.scenarioEs: Usen la historia de la misión para conectar la matemática con un problema real.

steps.en: Pick one practice problem and solve it on paper together.
steps.es: Elijan un problema de práctica y resuélvanlo en papel juntos.
steps.hintEs: Hablen de cada paso en voz alta.

steps.en: Check: does your answer follow — A ratio compares two quantities, and the order you write them in matters.?
steps.es: Verifiquen: ¿su respuesta sigue — A ratio compares two quantities, and the order you write them in matters.?
steps.hint: This is the flagship lesson's deepest idea.
steps.hintEs: Esta es la idea más profunda de la lección estrella.

say.en: What part of the mission story helps here?
say.es: ¿Qué parte de la historia de la misión ayuda aquí?

say.en: Let's re-read the key idea together.

dontSay.en: This flagship lesson is too hard — skip it.
dontSay.es: Esta lección estrella es muy difícil — sáltenla.
